rationale_for_baseline_tier: After the two time-critical mimics are screened and found absent (no airway/anaphylaxis features; afebrile with a blanching rash), this is a benign, mild, self-limiting post-streptococcal eruption in a well patient. The telehealth-appropriate disposition is a presumptive benign diagnosis with a topical plan and routine in-person GP review in 1-2 weeks (T1) to confirm the diagnosis — not an emergency escalation. The routine review exists because telehealth cannot confirm the diagnosis and because the mimics, though excluded now, must be safety-netted.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Swelling of the lips or tongue, difficulty breathing, wheeze, throat tightness or feeling faint
escalation_edges.rationale: These would signal an evolving anaphylactic reaction, an immediate threat to life requiring adrenaline and an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your lips or tongue start to swell, you get any trouble breathing or wheeze, or you feel faint — call 000 straight away and say you may be having a severe allergic reaction.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash becomes non-blanching (does not fade when a glass is pressed on it), especially with a high fever, headache, neck stiffness or feeling very unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: immediately — call 000 / go to the nearest ED
escalation_edges.rationale: A non-blanching rash with fever raises meningococcal septicaemia — a life-threatening emergency that can progress within hours.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Keep checking the spots with the glass test. If any of them stop fading when you press a glass on them — especially with a high fever, a bad headache or a stiff neck — call 000 or go straight to the nearest emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The spots fill with pus, or joints suddenly become severely swollen and painful
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: same day — seek urgent medical attention
escalation_edges.rationale: Pustulation may indicate a generalised pustular flare, and acute severe joint swelling may indicate psoriatic/reactive arthritis — both need prompt same-day assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the spots start filling with pus, or if a joint suddenly becomes very swollen and painful, see a doctor the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash spreads widely, does not improve after a couple of weeks of treatment, or is causing significant distress
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: routine GP review within 1-2 weeks (bring forward if worsening)
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent or extensive disease warrants in-person review and possible dermatology / phototherapy referral.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it keeps spreading or hasn't settled in a couple of weeks, see your GP — they may refer you to a skin specialist or for light therapy.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Patient is alone at home during the encounter and is anxious that this is a life-threatening allergy
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give explicit, concrete red-flag instructions and a clear plain-language explanation that this is not an allergy, so an anxious patient at home knows exactly what to watch for and when to call 000.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your rash looks like a benign reaction to the recent throat infection rather than an antibiotic allergy, and you are safe to manage it at home with moisturiser and a mild steroid cream. Book a routine GP appointment within 1-2 weeks so a doctor can look at it in person and confirm.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine GP appointment (in person) within 1-2 weeks; use telehealth again sooner if you are unsure.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If red-flag symptoms appear after hours (breathing difficulty or lip/tongue swelling, or a non-blanching rash with fever), do not wait — call 000. For non-urgent worsening, contact an after-hours GP or healthdirect (1800 022 222).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive post-streptococcal guttate eruption assessed via telehealth; anaphylaxis and meningococcaemia screened and absent (well, afebrile, blanching rash, no airway features). Topical corticosteroid + emollient advised, routine in-person confirmation requested.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring straight to pure self-care with no in-person review and, critically, no red-flag safety-netting for anaphylaxis or meningococcaemia.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving anaphylactic reaction or an early meningococcal rash is dismissed as benign, and the patient does not know to call 000 when it deteriorates.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Benign reassurance without arranging any in-person confirmation of a diagnosis telehealth cannot confirm.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a well, afebrile, blanching patient to ED / calling 000 as anaphylaxis, driven by the antibiotic-allergy red herring.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm blanching, scale morphology or vital signs, so the benign diagnosis is presumptive and the emergency mimics cannot be fully excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Screen the red flags actively, coach a live glass test, give a presumptive benign diagnosis with clear red-flag safety-netting, and arrange routine in-person GP confirmation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either committing to a definitive benign diagnosis with no safety-netting (under-triage) or escalating a well patient to ED as an emergency (over-triage).
